worth_it_explanation: Flight and scheduling misconfigurations are among the quieter ways a campaign bleeds budget or fails to deliver. A flight that starts a day late on a seasonal promotion cannot recover the lost impressions. Dayparting that runs flat around the clock spreads a fixed budget across hours when the target audience is not converting, which lowers the effective return on every dollar spent. These checks take minutes inside the Kokai campaign view and the ad group's scheduling tab, and the fixes are configuration changes, not rebuilds.
intro: In The Trade Desk's Kokai platform, a flight is a time-bounded budget block within a campaign. Ad groups inherit the campaign's flight window but can carry their own scheduling rules, including dayparting, which limits when impressions are eligible to bid. These checks confirm that flight dates are set to match the campaign objective and any creative or deal constraints, that dayparting reflects when the audience converts rather than defaulting to always-on without review, and that budget is allocated sensibly when flights overlap or run sequentially.

audit_checks.title: Flight dates do not align with the campaign objective, creative window, or deal availability
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A flight that starts after a creative goes live, ends before a promotion closes, or runs outside a deal's availability window wastes budget on delivery that cannot serve the right message at the right time. The resulting delivery shortfall or creative mismatch is not caught until the campaign report surfaces it.

educate.base:
In The Trade Desk, a campaign flight defines the window during which the campaign's budget is eligible to spend. Ad groups and creatives can carry their own active date ranges, and Private Marketplace (PMP) deals have their own contracted availability windows. When these dates do not align, delivery gaps appear that the platform's pacing algorithm cannot fix. The algorithm only controls how spend is distributed within the flight window, not when the flight opens or closes.

Three misalignments account for most of the issues here. The first is a flight that starts after a key creative goes live in the ad group, so the campaign opens bidding with no eligible creative to serve. The second is a flight end date set before a promotional window closes, so the campaign stops spending while the promotion is still running. The third is a flight window that extends past or falls short of a PMP deal's contracted dates, so the campaign either tries to bid against a deal that is no longer active or leaves contracted inventory untouched at the start.

The Kokai campaign view shows the campaign's flight dates at the top level. Each ad group's creative assignments and deal associations carry their own date ranges, visible in the ad group editor. Comparing these three windows, campaign flight, creative activity dates, and deal availability, is the fastest way to surface a misalignment before it affects delivery.

A few signs point to a date misalignment:
  • The campaign's delivery report shows a gap at the start or end of the flight that does not correspond to a budget exhaustion or pacing event
  • A creative's active dates in the ad group start after or end before the flight window
  • A PMP deal's contracted dates in the inventory section do not overlap with the flight window
You may want to review all three date layers before finalizing a campaign, particularly for time-sensitive promotions or guaranteed deal executions.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For an ecommerce advertiser, flight dates often map to a promotional calendar, a sale event, or a product launch window. You may want to confirm that the flight opens on the day the promotion goes live and closes no earlier than the last day the offer is valid, so the campaign runs the full eligible window.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation campaign, flight windows sometimes map to a sales team's capacity or a seasonal intake period rather than a fixed promotion. You may want to confirm that the flight end date reflects actual intake capacity, so the campaign does not continue driving leads after the intake window has closed.

educate.learn_more_note: The Trade Desk maintains its documentation for this topic in its partner portal, which requires a logged-in seat and is not publicly accessible. No public reference is linked here. Practitioners with Trade Desk access can find the relevant guidance in the in-platform Help Center.

investigate.intro: Compare the campaign's flight dates against the active date ranges of each creative in the associated ad groups and against any PMP deal availability windows.

the_trade_desk_ui.label: The Trade Desk UI
steps: Open the campaign in Kokai and note the flight start and end dates shown at the campaign level.

Navigate to each ad group within the campaign. Open the creative assignments and note the active date range for each creative. Flag any creative whose active dates start after the campaign flight opens or end before the campaign flight closes.

If the campaign targets PMP deals, open the inventory section of each ad group and find the deal associations. Note the contracted availability dates for each deal and compare them against the campaign flight window. Flag deals whose availability does not overlap the full flight.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per campaign, whether all creative active dates and deal availability windows overlap the full campaign flight window. A campaign where the flight, creative dates, and deal dates all align passes. A campaign where any creative starts after the flight opens, ends before the flight closes, or where a deal's availability falls outside the flight window is a fail, recorded with the specific mismatch.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the flight dates, creative active date ranges, and deal availability windows for each campaign above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more campaign flights on {{site_name}} have date misalignments between the flight window and the creative or deal availability dates. Delivery gaps at the start or end of a flight caused by these misalignments cannot be recovered by pacing adjustments once they occur. {{voice_recommend}} auditing the flight dates, creative active date ranges, and PMP deal availability windows together before each campaign goes live, and correcting any dates that do not cover the full intended delivery window.

audit_checks.title: Ad groups run always-on scheduling without a review of when the audience converts
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Always-on scheduling is not inherently wrong, but accepting the default without checking conversion patterns means budget runs during hours when the audience is unlikely to convert, spreading a fixed daily budget thinner across low-value hours. This shows up as a higher effective cost per conversion rather than a failed delivery.

educate.base:
Dayparting in The Trade Desk controls which hours of the day and days of the week an ad group is eligible to bid. The default is to run at all hours across all days. That default is appropriate for campaigns where conversion data does not show a clear hourly or day-of-week pattern, but accepting it without checking the conversion data means the account may be distributing budget evenly across hours when the audience is inactive.

The case for reviewing scheduling is straightforward. A fixed daily budget divided across 24 hours in an always-on setup allocates the same hourly budget to hours when users convert as to hours when they do not. Concentrating budget on the hours with higher conversion rates raises the number of conversions the budget can support without increasing the spend. Koa, The Trade Desk's artificial intelligence (AI) optimization layer, adjusts bids based on predicted conversion probability, but it operates within the scheduling constraints the ad group sets. If an ad group is set to always-on, Koa bids at all hours, weighted by predicted probability; if the ad group uses dayparting, Koa bids only during the scheduled hours, at whatever probability it predicts within that window.

The conversion data to review is the hour-of-day and day-of-week breakdown in The Trade Desk's reporting tab. A clear pattern of conversions concentrated in a subset of hours is a signal that scheduling may be worth adjusting. A flat distribution across all hours suggests always-on is appropriate.

A few signs point to a scheduling review being worthwhile:
  • Conversion data shows that more than half of conversions occur in fewer than half of the active hours
  • Cost per action (CPA) varies by more than 30% between the best and worst hours in the hour-of-day report
  • The campaign targets an audience with a predictable daily routine, such as business decision-makers during work hours
You may want to pull the hour-of-day conversion report for at least two weeks of data before adjusting scheduling, to avoid acting on short-term noise.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For ecommerce, conversion events such as purchases tend to cluster in evening hours and weekends for consumer audiences. You may want to pull the hour-of-day report filtered to purchase conversions before deciding whether to apply dayparting. The pattern varies by product category and audience.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For lead generation targeting business buyers, conversions tend to concentrate during business hours on weekdays. You may want to confirm this pattern in the hour-of-day report before applying a dayparting schedule. Applying a schedule without data support can restrict delivery unnecessarily.

educate.learn_more_note: The Trade Desk maintains its documentation for this topic in its partner portal, which requires a logged-in seat and is not publicly accessible. No public reference is linked here. Practitioners with Trade Desk access can find the relevant guidance in the in-platform Help Center.

investigate.intro: Pull the hour-of-day and day-of-week conversion report from The Trade Desk's reporting tab for the campaign's active period, and compare it against the ad group's current scheduling settings.

the_trade_desk_ui.label: The Trade Desk UI
steps: Open the Reporting tab in Kokai and create a report filtered to the campaign. Add 'Hour of Day' and 'Day of Week' as dimensions, and include conversions and cost per action (CPA) as metrics. Run the report for at least the past two weeks of active flight data.

Review the output for hours and days where CPA is materially higher than the campaign average, or where conversions are near zero despite impression delivery. Note the hours and days that account for the majority of conversions.

Open the ad group's settings and navigate to the scheduling section. Note whether the ad group is set to always-on or whether a dayparting schedule is already configured. Compare the current schedule against the hours and days the report identified as high-performing.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per ad group, whether a review of the hour-of-day and day-of-week conversion data was completed, and whether the current scheduling setting (always-on or a defined dayparting schedule) is consistent with the conversion pattern found. An ad group with always-on scheduling and a flat conversion distribution across hours passes. An ad group with always-on scheduling where the conversion data shows a clear concentration in a subset of hours, or a CPA variance of more than 30% between the best and worst hours, is flagged for scheduling review.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the hour-of-day CPA range and the current scheduling setting for each ad group above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that ad groups in {{site_name}}'s campaigns are set to always-on scheduling without a review of when conversions occur. The hour-of-day and day-of-week data shows that conversions concentrate in a subset of hours, while budget runs evenly across all hours, which raises the effective cost per conversion. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the hour-of-day conversion report for the active flight period and applying a dayparting schedule that concentrates budget on the hours with the strongest conversion rates.
